serving.natural_language_portion: One medium Missi Roti weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Missi Roti (Gram flour chapati) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for besan (gram flour) chapati / roti. Missi Roti is a spiced variant of besan roti made with a blend of gram flour and whole wheat flour with onion, carom seeds, and coriander. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN besan roti data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for gram flour (chickpea flour, SR Legacy) and whole wheat flatbread to produce a blended per-100g estimate representative of a 50:50 gram flour to wheat flour ratio with standard spicing. Energy and macros are consistent with published Indian dietary literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Missi Roti was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated by analogy with chapati made from a blend of gram flour and whole wheat flour. Chapati (whole wheat) has a reported GI of approximately 52–62 (Atkinson et al. 2021; Foster-Powell et al. 2002). The inclusion of gram flour (besan, GI ~28–35 per Sydney GI Database for chickpea-based products) lowers the blended GI. A conservative estimate of GI 52 is used, representing the lower bound of whole wheat chapati and consistent with the high legume-flour content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 40.0) / 100 = 20.8 for an 80 g serving providing approximately 40 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.guidance: Missi Roti contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional salt or high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys to keep daily sodium within the recommended 1500–2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 52 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 20.8 per 80 g serving, Missi Roti is preferable to refined wheat bread for blood glucose management, but portion control remains important as the glycemic load per serving is moderate; pairing with a protein or vegetable side dish can further blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary fibre and legume flour content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high gram flour content of Missi Roti contributes approximately 5.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g and a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total fat intake if ghee is used liberally in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Missi Roti is a traditional North Indian flatbread, particularly popular in Punjab and Rajasthan, made from a blend of gram flour (besan) and whole wheat flour (atta) combined with finely chopped onions, carom seeds (ajwain), fresh coriander, green chilli, and salt. The dough is kneaded with water and a small amount of oil, then rolled into round discs and cooked on a dry iron griddle (tawa) until golden-brown spots appear on both sides, often finished with a light brushing of ghee or butter. The resulting bread has a dense, slightly chewy texture with a nutty, earthy flavour from the gram flour and a mild spiced aroma from the ajwain and coriander. Missi Roti is a protein-enriched flatbread compared to plain wheat roti, providing approximately 10.5 g protein and 5.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it nutritionally superior to refined-flour breads. Its lower glycemic index (estimated GI ~52) relative to white bread makes it a preferred carbohydrate source for individuals managing blood glucose. In Singapore, Missi Roti is served at North Indian restaurants such as Mr Tandoor and Kinara at Boat Quay, typically as an accompaniment to dal, paneer dishes, or curries. Clinically, the high legume-flour content supports cardiovascular health through soluble fibre and plant protein, while the moderate sodium content warrants attention for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: What flour is used in Missi Roti?
Is Missi Roti good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Missi Roti as a named dish was found; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN besan roti data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central chickpea flour and whole wheat flatbread entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on chapati and chickpea-flour GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN besan roti and USDA FoodData Central chickpea flour (SR Legacy) and whole wheat flatbread data, assuming a representative 50:50 gram flour to whole wheat flour ratio with standard spicing and minimal ghee. Actual restaurant servings may vary in fat content depending on ghee usage. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as Missi Roti is a plant-based flatbread containing no animal-derived ingredients in its base formulation; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) for the base recipe without industrially hydrogenated fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central flatbread data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the base recipe contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this food category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Missi Roti was found in the University of Sydney GI Database at time of processing. 8. is_vegan set to false as ghee or butter is commonly used in preparation; is_vegetarian true as no meat is present. 9. FoodOn match type is closest_parent as no exact FoodOn term for Missi Roti exists. 10. FoodEx2 code A000E (Flatbreads) is used as closest parent; no specific Missi Roti FoodEx2 entry exists.
